title: Pelau
subtitle: Trinidadian one-pot rice with pigeon peas and chicken
excerpt: Chicken browned in burned (caramelised) sugar, then cooked with pigeon peas, coconut milk, and rice in a single pot until the rice has absorbed all the liquid and the bottom layer has formed a lightly caramelised crust. Pelau is Trinidad's definitive one-pot dish, the national equivalent of rice and peas, but richer, with the burned-sugar base giving a bitter-sweet depth that sets it apart from all other rice dishes.
sourceNotes: Pelau has roots in both the Spanish paella tradition (via colonial contact) and in the West African one-pot rice-and-protein dishes brought by enslaved people. The burned-sugar technique for browning meat is specifically Trinidadian and appears in Trinidadian cookbooks from the early 20th century. Pigeon peas are the traditional pulse, though kidney beans can be substituted.

content.text: The burned sugar step is the key to pelau. Caster sugar is added to a hot dry pot and allowed to caramelise until it is dark brown, significantly darker than ordinary caramel, and slightly bitter. The chicken goes in while the sugar is still liquid, and the bitter-sweet coating it forms is what gives pelau its characteristic flavour. Do not be afraid to take the sugar dark: a pale caramel produces an insipid pelau.

The ratio of liquid to rice determines whether the pelau will be moist or dry. Trinidadians often prefer a slightly wet pelau; others prefer the rice completely dry with a pressed crust at the bottom. Use 1.5 parts liquid to 1 part rice and resist the urge to stir after the rice has been added.

Green seasoning note: Trinidadian green seasoning is a blended paste of coriander, shadow beni (culantro), spring onion, garlic, and scotch bonnet. If unavailable, use a blend of fresh coriander, garlic, spring onion, and scotch bonnet.
Burn the sugar and brown the chicken

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} with {{green-seasoning}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} and leave for 15 minutes. Heat a large heavy pot over medium-high heat. Add the {{caster-sugar}} and swirl the pan. When the sugar has melted and turned dark brown (almost burnt), add the chicken pieces immediately. They will spit and sizzle violently, take care. Brown the chicken on all sides in the caramelised sugar, about 3 minutes per side. Remove the chicken and set aside.

Add the {{garlic}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{thyme}}, and {{allspice}} to the pot. Stir for 1 minute. Return the chicken. Add the drained {{pigeon-peas}}, {{coconut-milk}}, {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole), and 400 ml of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer. Cover and cook for 20 minutes. Add the {{long-grain-rice}}, ensuring it is submerged in the liquid (add a little more water if needed to just cover). Cover tightly and cook on the lowest heat for 20 minutes without lifting the lid. Rest covered for 10 minutes. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving.

items.fix: The sugar should be dark brown, not black. Once smoking starts, add the chicken immediately. If too bitter, discard and restart, burnt sugar past the dark-brown stage is unusable
items.cause: Caramelised too far before chicken added
items.symptom: Sugar burns to black and tastes acrid

items.fix: Do not lift the lid during the rice-cooking phase. Use no more than 1.5 parts liquid to 1 part rice
items.cause: Too much liquid or lid opened during cooking

items.fix: The bottom crust (concon) is traditional and welcome in small amounts. If the smell is burning rather than toasting, reduce the heat
items.cause: Heat too high during the rice phase
items.symptom: Rice sticking and burning at the bottom

content.text: Pelau is Trinidad's one-pot national dish, made for family gatherings, cricket matches, and Sunday dinners. The burned-sugar technique for browning meat connects it to similar techniques across the Caribbean and in the American South. In Trinidad pelau is made at the beach, at the savannah on Divali night, at christenings and wakes, a food of communal occasions. The bottom crust (concon) that forms when the rice is cooked low is fought over: the caramelised layer is considered the best part.
